rationale: The citing paper explicitly summarizes Swait and Erdem (2002) as showing that inconsistent SKU availability affects systematic utility, and uses that point in its core literature framing on OOS effects. This is not just a bibliography-list mention: it helps justify modeling OOS as utility-relevant and motivates the paper's contribution claims. The authors then position their work as going beyond prior studies by using household-occasion OOS data and estimating additional intertemporal and contextual OOS effects.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Swait and Erdem's availability-consistency insight by extending the line of inquiry to recurring OOS with household-level purchase-occasion data and a richer utility specification. It positions its contribution as improved identification and broader managerial implications relative to earlier availability-consistency studies.
